title: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain
display_title: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain
wikitext:
[]: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain             Quote Post by Voyager1 » Mon Jan 20, 2025 10:26 pm Gendo Ikari, the Mastermind, The Conspirator, the Puppeteer behind all things in NERV, is not only one of the worst father figures in the whole of anime next to Fullmetal Alchemist's The Father, but is also one of the most compelling if not one of the most horrifying villains in all of fiction, This may seem like a tall order, but allow me to shed light on the subject, and at the end of the analysis, I believe you might be compelled to agree. First off, Shinji Ikari, the main character of the series, whose shoes we as the audience are thrust headlong into, come face to face with his father, one who for reasons unknown to him as they are to us, abandoned him when he needed him the most, only for Gendo to turn the idea on it's head, as if he is the one who needs Shinji, in this case, to pilot the Evangelion Unit. It wasn't enough that his attempts to defy Gendo's order fell upon deaf ears, but he purposefully guilted the young boy into piloting the Unit by showing him an injured Rei. Worse still, none of the NERV officials, including Misato, who upon Gendo's orders, brought the boy to him, thinking it was for the better, that Rei was a mere clone, and thus negating the severity of the situation, as Gendo could've easily called on another to pilot. This meant that Shinji was never truly needed as a pilot, a devastating revelation that came to him much later in the series once his form of validation, the only means he thought people would acknowledge his presence, treat him like a human being, and grant him the appreciation he desires for so long, was ultimately fruitless. Gendo manipulated Shinji, knowing the Rei clones were as disposable to him as Shinji. The only problem is, he loved Rei more as a daughter than Shinji as a son, so it's no wonder Shinji's envy and need for validation grew as the original series went on. Before the events of the original series, Yui, Gendo's wife, willingly gave her life to power the EVA Unit, transfusing her soul into the core to power it, with the desire to give her son the future she wanted for him. Unfortunately, Gendo's will was beyond weak, as he was willing to do anything to see Yui again, even if it meant defying her most cherished wish, even if it mean traumatizing her son beyond all known boundaries, even if it meant mankind's extinction. And for that reason exactly, SEELE recruited him as NERV's commander after Geher was dissolved. Not only was Gendo a master manipulator, as well as a cunning tactician, but he too as as much a puppet as he was the puppeteer. SEELE used the Human Instrumentality Project as a bargaining chip to keep Gendo under their command, just as he did NERV. As the series went on, their grip on Gendo slipped further, as he sought to hijack the project, thus enacting SEELE to betray humanity by targeting NERV as a Rogue Organization. The battle against the Angels was rigged from the beginning. NERV, parading the organization as humanity's best, last, stand against the Angels. A mere facade. Why else would Gendo and SEELE sabotage Jet Alone? To discredit the project and make their own seem more credible. Because with the technology they had, they never truly needed teenage pilots to save the world; as had ulterior motives, not to save humanity, but to merge it into the consciousness of a superbeing: a state of existence that can only be described as endless limbo; Instrumentality. All in the name of a prophecy from the Dead Sea Scrolls. The irony is that the Scrolls only became a self-fulfilling prophecy when SEELE, an organization that has existence since their discovery, enacted their plans to construct the EVA units for the purpose of fulfilling Instrumentality as opposed to merely protecting humanity. With all that power cumulated to a single individual, Gendo Ikari had acquired the power of the Gods, and had the ability to shape reality to his will beyond the scope of what's known as humanly possible. For Shinji, a boy who has been betrayed by his father in both the Originals and the Rebuilds, having to live with the fact that the one person you looked up to to raise you, turned his back for the most inhuman reasons conceivable, and willing to stop at nothing until the fabric of reality is his to shape, makes a villain as horrifyingly cosmic as deeply personal; a double edged sword. Gendo knows all of Shinji's weaknesses and strengths, and unjustly punishes him for disobeying orders to break down his will bit by bit until it is replaced by Gendo's own will. The fact that their persona's mirror each other is just as terrifying as well; as both Gendo and Shinji are a mirror into each other. How can one possibly beat a person who knows everything about you and is willing to use it to your advantage? Much less a person who you grew up very close to? There's a reason why Shinji was able to recuperate in Village-3. A passage from Carl Jung which states, "These are the central secrets of men, that they feel themselves failures as men, that is as person who happen to be men, that they are torn between fear and rage, and that they are emotionally dependent but resentful of the object of that dependence. Again, the only way out is consciously to acknowledge these insufferable truths. They must begin with themselves and then share that truth with others, not with a woman, but with another man. That man, also caught in a fearful defendeness, may scorn the truth teller; his scorn in proportion to his fear, but he may also step from behind the ramparts to acknowledge his brother." As was the case in the End of Evangelion, Shinji was torn between fear and rage, just as Gendo and SEELE wanted him to be; they willed him to be complacent in the face of adversity, in the face of depression, self pity, and most of all the desires to not exist. Shinji himself was faced with this dilemma, as these were the expectations that he felt burdened with, of which Gendo himself was unwilling to acknowledge within himself. It is an odd thing how plenty of people within the fandom like to criticize Shinji for his sensitive nature and meek approach to things, when these are traits that Gendo was looking for to create the perfect obedient child soldier. Gendo was Shinji's shadow, his parental archetype, the one who represented Shinji's patriarchal qualities of his persona, of which Gendo himself knew, and thus exerted his will over him to make Shinji's will his will. It just so happened this one was the perfect sync for EVA Unit 01, having the desire to reunite with his own mother no doubt, as her soul powered the core in a twist of fate. Some people often overlook the traumas that child soldiers throughout history have faced, including that of the First World War. The novel "All Quiet on the Western Front" is a prime example of the facade of courageous and unyielding masculinity that is portrayed on the warfront that cowardly generals who rest on their velvet chairs would be more than willing to persuade the gullible into throwing away their lives. As such, I parallel this phenomenon to Shinji's sudden reluctant recruitment. Shinji had every right to be afraid of going to war, being inexperienced and head thrown into the meat-grinder no doubt, a human being who thought he could find acknowledgement and validation of his existence in being a pilot, forced to fight cosmic threats, reduced to a mere pawn in a bigger game. His reaction to all things that occurred were nothing less than justified, but SEELE wanted to desensitize him. Seeing how he was treated more as a tool than a human, it is little wonder why he was desperate for validation not just from his father, but from others who he looked up to to care for him; Misato being one. And if that very leader happened to be your father, of whom your own personality was built upon...it would make it all the more terrifying. In Village-3, away from Gendo's reach, Shinji no longer felt the need to uphold the facade nor the expectations his father burdened him with during the beginning of the war. His frail, traumatized, love-starved self was laid bare. Except instead of SEELE launching an all out attack, the scene was peaceful; a much needed respite from the madness that engulfed the planet for the past 14 years, while the others who bore the "Curse of Evangelion" were unable to grow, so long as they kept piloting the EVA's, the incarnation of the mother's womb. Thus, after much needed help from friends who had never forgotten him, Shinji found it in himself to face his father as he is, and defied his orders. The very figure who knew too much and mentally enslaved the boy, now faced a challenge. Shinji did this, knowing the face of reality itself rested on his shoulders, and was facing a Lovecraftian threat greater than anything thought possible. Until Gendo realized his efforts to reunite with Yui were a sunk cost fallacy, he wasn't willing to ever admit he was in the wrong. He was a slave to his own desires and ambition as Shinji was a slave to his oppression and manipulation, a shallow empty man with seemingly no willpower over himself, dared to shape the universe to his willpower. And it took more willpower than anyone in NERV combined to face such a threat, one which Shinji found within himself in the safety of Village-3 by, in the words of Jung, "Acknowledging these insufferable truths", as Shinji felt dependent and resentful of the object of his dependance.

[]: Re: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain             Quote Post by gloomyAmateur » Thu Jan 23, 2025 2:36 pm And then there's manga Gendo, of which his sole desire is to become a God. He is so much more cruel, callous and foreboding in Sadamato's interpretation, at the cost of him having less depth.

[]: Re: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain             Quote Post by Voyager1 » Mon Jan 27, 2025 6:08 pm gloomyAmateur wrote: And then there's manga Gendo, of which his sole desire is to become a God. He is so much more cruel, callous and foreboding in Sadamato's interpretation, at the cost of him having less depth.

[]: Re: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain             Quote Post by Weird_ocean » Tue Jan 28, 2025 12:05 am In NGE and EOE Gendo was a perfect representation of Nietzschean Ubermensch archetype: a man not bound by religious or societal moral norms. A person who, who can create his own morals, or follow existing ones, but only if it benefits him and his goals. A man with a strong vision of the future, and an absolute certainty, that what he does is right, no matter the cost. His actions come not form a place of resentment or malice, but from a pure desire to change the world they way he think is the best. In EOE when his plan failed, he admits defeat, with no sign of anger or resentment, and accepts his fate. Rebuild Gendo is different. He is swayed by emotions, when Rei pushes him to a dinner with his son. He shows resentment towards Shinji, when he says "That's why I hate kids.". In his memories, we see him resenting the world and people around him. He does cruel things to Shinji to simply make him suffer, when he sends Rei lookalike his way, for her to die in front of Shinji. (judging by what Fuyutsuki said) Gendo also simply abandons his lifelong path of achieving his goal, that he sacrificed everything for, by a very primitive and vulgar realization, that his son has half of his wife's DNA, something that even children in the first grade already know. In other words, Rebuild Gendo, from a man above good and evil in NGE, was reduced to your typical villain that hates the world.

[]: Re: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain             Quote Post by Voyager1 » Tue Feb 04, 2025 9:11 pm Also, I could make the argument that, as foreboding and godlike his archetype of the Nietzchian Ubermench is, it's just that. In Jungian, terms, a persona, similar to how Asuka's aggressive, rebellious, and disrespectful persona masks her true self, or how Shinji's passive, complacent, and indecisive persona masks his true self. Gendo is only able to portray this persona of his so long as he remains in control of the situation, so long as he had complete control. As things start spiraling out of control, he grows more desperate, the more this persona starts to crumble. Even in Terminal Dogma, cold as he is, he begs Rei to come back when she defies his orders to help the vulnerable and mentally deteriorating Shinji. In the Rebuilds, this persona of his collapses entirely when he comes faced with his own son, of whom he tried everything within his power to traumatize him into submission to become like him; the son suffering the sins of the father, his parental archetype. At the end of everything, Gendo is still human, thus breaking the archetype. As for Mari, she is somewhat of a ghost, from a third party unknown even to SEELE, which for all intents and purposes behind the curtain, is a good thing. Against an enemy that knows too much, and knows your every weakness and is willing to exploit it to use trauma based manipulation tactics to force your hand into becoming a complacent pilot, what better way to fight back than as a person, should not exist? My theory is that Mari's former sensei, Futsuyuki, knew of the impending danger she was in once she was selected as part of the EVA Project, and thus erased all records of her existence. She is quite literally, a wrench thrown into SEELE and Gendo's plans, as she is the only person that actually enjoys piloting an EVA, whereas for the rest of the pilots, it is a burden for them to carry against their will, as they've been conditioned to believe just as the rest of the staff of NERV that this is for the sake of humanity's best interests. Thus, Mari turns SEELE's greatest weapon against them, not the EVA itself, but it's original purpose; a prison for the pilot.

Article: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain
GE and EOE Gendo was a perfect representation of Nietzschean Ubermensch archetype: a man not bound by religious or societal moral norms. A person who, who can create his own morals, or follow existing ones, but only if it benefits him and his goals. A man with a strong vision of the future, and an absolute certainty, that what he does is right, no matter the cost. His actions come not form a place of resentment or malice, but from a pure desire to change the world they way he think is the best. In EOE when his plan failed, he admits defeat, with no sign of anger or resentment, and accepts his fate. Rebuild Gendo is different. He is swayed by emotions, when Rei pushes him to a dinner with his son. He shows resentment towards Shinji, when he says "That's why I hate kids.". In his memories, we see him resenting the world and people around him. He does cruel things to Shinji to simply make him suffer, when he sends Rei lookalike his way, for her to die in front of Shinji. (judging by what Fuyutsuki said) Gendo also simply abandons his lifelong path of achieving his goal, that he sacrificed everything for, by a very primitive and vulgar realization, that his son has half of his wife's DNA, something that even children in the first grade already know. In other words, Rebuild Gendo, from a man above good and evil in NGE, was reduced to your typical villain that hates the world.

He does cruel things to Shinji to simply make him suffer, when he sends Rei lookalike his way, for her to die in front of Shinji. (judging by what Fuyutsuki said) G

Article: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain
 and people around him. He does cruel things to Shinji to simply make him suffer, when he sends Rei lookalike his way, for her to die in front of Shinji. (judging by what Fuyutsuki said) Gendo also simply abandons his lifelong path of achieving his goal, that he sacrificed everything for, by a very primitive and vulgar realization, that his son has half of his wife's DNA, something that even children in the first grade already know. In other words, Rebuild Gendo, from a man above good and evil in NGE, was reduced to your typical villain that hates the world.

[]: Re: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain             Quote Post by Voyager1 » Tue Feb 04, 2025 9:11 pm Also, I could make the argument that, as foreboding and godlike his archetype of the Nietzchian Ubermench is, it's just that. In Jungian, terms, a persona, similar to how Asuka's aggressive, rebellious, and disrespectful persona masks her true self, or how Shinji's passive, complacent, and indecisive persona masks his true self. Gendo is only able to portray this persona of his so long as he remains in control of the situation, so long as he had complete control. As things start spiraling out of control, he grows more desperate, the more this persona starts to crumble. Even in Terminal Dogma, cold as he is, he begs Rei to come back when she defies his orders to help the vulnerable and mentally deteriorating Shinji. In the Rebuilds, this persona of his collapses entirely when he comes faced with his own son, of whom he tried everything within his power to traumatize him into submission to become like him; the son suffering the sins of the father, his parental archetype. At the end of everything, Gendo is still human, thus breaking the archetype. As for Mari, she is somewhat of a ghost, from a third party unknown even to SEELE, which for all intents and purposes behind the curtain, is a good thing. Against an enemy that knows too much, and knows your every weakness and is willing to exploit it to use trauma based manipulation tactics to force your hand into becoming a complacent pilot, what better way to fight back than as a person, should not exist? My theory is that Mari's former sensei, Futsuyuki, knew of the impending danger

Article: What Makes Gendo a Perfect Villain
 trauma based manipulation tactics to force your hand into becoming a complacent pilot, what better way to fight back than as a person, should not exist? My theory is that Mari's former sensei, Futsuyuki, knew of the impending danger she was in once she was selected as part of the EVA Project, and thus erased all records of her existence. She is quite literally, a wrench thrown into SEELE and Gendo's plans, as she is the only person that actually enjoys piloting an EVA, whereas for the rest of the pilots, it is a burden for them to carry against their will, as they've been conditioned to believe just as the rest of the staff of NERV that this is for the sake of humanity's best interests. Thus, Mari turns SEELE's greatest weapon against them, not the EVA itself, but it's original purpose; a prison for the pilot.
